final_answer: Large language models (LLMs) are a type of AI system trained on massive text datasets, enabling them to generate and work with human language. They can perform a wide range of language tasks, such as answering questions, summarizing text, translating between languages, writing content, and reasoning through complex problems. LLMs have various applications in fields like chatbots, content generation, and enterprise use cases. However, they also come with risks such as data quality issues, model bias, and the potential for misinformation.

history.thought: To find the definition of a LLM, I need to understand what it stands for and its general concept. It's likely related to artificial intelligence or computer science.
history.observation:
The University of Arizona Wordmark Line Logo White. 1. University of Arizona Libraries. # What is a large language model (LLM)? A large language model (LLM) is a type of AI system trained on massive text datasets, often billions of words, that can generate and work with human language. By analyzing all this data, the model learns patterns and structures of language, enabling it to predict and generate plausible text. LLMs can perform a wide range of language tasks, such as answering questions, summarizing text, translating between languages, writing content, and reasoning through complex, multi-step problems. Well-known AI products powered by LLMs include ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, Microsoft Copilot, and Meta AI. You may also hear the term "foundation model." This refers to a large pretrained model that serves as a base for many different downstream tasks and applications. Many of today's foundation models are also multimodal, meaning they can work with images, audio, and video in addition to text.

Large language models (LLMs) are a category of [deep learning](https://www.ibm.com/think/topics/deep-learning) models trained on immense amounts of data, making them capable of understanding and generating natural language and other types of content to perform a wide range of tasks. LLMs can have billions or trillions of these weights, which are one type of [LLM parameter](https://www.ibm.com/think/topics/llm-parameters), the internal configuration variables of a machine learning model that control how it processes data and makes predictions. To further refine models, data scientists often use [reinforcement learning from human feedback](https://www.ibm.com/think/topics/rlhf) (RLHF), a form of fine-tuning where humans rank model outputs and the model is trained to prefer outputs that humans rank higher. Such tasks don’t always have abundant labeled data, so [reinforcement learning](https://www.ibm.com/think/topics/reinforcement-learning) is often used in the creation of [reasoning model](https://www.ibm.com/think/topics/reasoning-model)s, LLMs that have been fine-tuned to break complex problems into smaller steps, often called “reasoning traces,” prior to generating a final output.

history.observation:
Applications powered by large language models (LLMs) are garnering a lot of success and attention in a wide array of fields. LLM apps are specialized platforms that can help developers handle the complexity of LLM-based features in their applications. This article aims to dive deeper into their functionality in the context of LLM application development while also discussing some specific platforms. In this stage, data required for fine-tuning the LLM is gathered, cleaned, and prepared. This stage is vital since the quality of the data used directly impacts the performance of the LLM. Here, the cleaned data is annotated with information such as metadata and semantic information so that the models can learn them during the training stage. Some LLM app platforms and data collection and preparation options are mentioned below. LangChain is an LLM app platform that supports building data connectors and pipelines to process data continuously.

# LLM apps: Real-world use cases, core building blocks and risks to know. * LLM apps now extend far beyond chat, combining models with retrieval, tools, memory and workflow automation to support real enterprise use cases. * Core building blocks include model selection, RAG infrastructure, orchestration frameworks, fine-tuning, evaluation and strong governance. These systems are software applications built on top of large language models to perform generative, conversational, analytical or decision-making tasks. Production LLM apps connect models to external data sources, tools, APIs, memory systems and business workflows so they can operate as part of a larger system rather than as isolated chatbots. It covers the major categories of LLM applications, the most common use cases across industries, the core building blocks that make these systems work and the key risks teams must address when deploying them in production. LLM apps can readily handle many of the same tasks as chatbots, but they also have a number of more sophisticated capabilities.
